comment_teaching:
Unlike many other COMP courses in UST that focus on theory, the instructor mainly puts his focus on the real-world use cases. His teaching style avoids dense mathematical proofs in favour of actionable insights (for example, he would go onto Etherscan to show a random transaction to demonstrate lecture content)

If you want to dive deep into the cryptographic primitives like SHA256 or merkle trees, or the theory behind consensus algorithms like Paxos or Nakamoto, then this course might not be the best choice for you.

Imo, if you know nothing about blockchain and you're curious to know more about it (as a beginner course), this course is a great choice for you.
comment_workload:
Lightweight if you compare it to other courses like COMP3711 (just my opinion because I'm not really good at all those theory stuff).

Overall only 4 assignments, 1 individual project, and 1 open-book final exam.

No tutorials or whatsoever, and the professor replies to your email really quickly if you have any difficulties or confusion regarding lecture materials.

No attendance, which is probably the reason why not many students attended the class (it's my lunch time for this semester T.T).

Have lecture recordings, meaning you can skip the lectures if you want (I'm not encouraging you to do it tho :] )

The assignments are lightweight (just to test your basic understanding of the materials taught within the past 2-3 weeks), which could probably be done within an hour.

The project basically asks you to make a simple DApp from scratch, for which you can ask for assistance from AI. Imo, since AI is so powerful these days, thinking about your DApp idea (whether it's innovative or useful) would be more important than the programming part itself.

The final exam is open book, which means you can print all the lecture notes into the exam, and almost all answers in the exam can be found in the lecture materials. If you read through all the lecture materials in detail before the exam, it shouldn't be too challenging for you.

Hope this review helps.

comment_content:
Most fruitful & enjoyable course content from the UST, especially if you are a crypto geek like me.

All lecture recording can be found on professor's YouTube channel. There are some required / recommended readings that is somewhat essential for your project / exam preparation (ZKP MOOC lecture 1 for exam).

Taught topics (in general)
- Cryptographic primitives
    - hash function
    - Diffie-Hellman key exchange
    - asymmetric encryption (RSA)
    - digital signature
    - basic number theory
- Bitcoin, Cryptocurrencies and Decentralization
    - PoW and idea of blockchain
    - payment channels
- Ethereum and Smart Contracts
    - Solidity / smart contract
    - vulnerabilities (frontrunning, reentrancy, etc.)
- Advanced Topics
    - Shamir's secret sharing
    - PoS
    - zero-knowledge proof

List of Topics (from syllabus):
1. Introduction to Cryptocurrencies and Decentralization
2. Hash Functions and Public-key Cryptography
3. The Double-spending Problem
4. Bitcoin and Proof-of-Work (PoW)
5. Proof-of-stake and other alternatives to PoW
6. Programmable Blockchains
7. Introduction to Ethereum and Solidity
8. Tools for Implementing Smart Contracts
9. Commitment Schemes
10. Auctions and Escrows
11. Re-entrancy and Out-of-gas Vulnerabilities
12. Incentivization Bugs and Attacks by Miners
13. Verifying Correctness of Smart Contracts

+ guest lectures on basic number theory & lightning network (L2) was provided by fellows from the prof's team

// Personal comment
As the other reviewer mentioned, this course teaches many things that "could be" used for illegal stuff. And many examples in the course / past paper are on some disastrous / trustless assumptions (e.g. mafia boss preventing internal murder). GenAI may decline to provide solutions for the past paper, due to ethical reasons. It told me "I am not able to provide information that could be used to facilitate illegal or unethical activities. My purpose is to help you understand the vulnerabilities of the blockchain casino contract and to encourage you to develop secure and responsible smart contracts."

But we are harmless CS students! Trust us! (never trust anyone)
comment_teaching:
Prof. Amir's teaching is great, as other reviews have mentioned. He also replies to students' emails quickly and clearly. This course would be a rare opportunity to learn from and interact with the expert who is very active in the field.

Due to anarchistic & volatile nature of cryptocurrency, some of the remarks professor make during the lecture can be quite humorous. (Don't buy crypto; this isn't an investment advice; what if the government does...)

As most homework are worded and cannot be auto-graded, I truly admire the TAs' hard work on grading those HW, project, and the exam. After some conversation or a few looks on their paper, I feel that the entire teaching team is really genius.

Note: you can assume the lab session to be another lecture session.
comment_workload:
The workload for this course isn't very light, especially if you had small or no experience on cryptography / zero-knowledge proof and cryptocurrency etc.

I had a little background on cryptocurrency and use of cryptographic hash functions, but not so much on proofs. If you have taken / taking 2711/3711 or some cybersecurity course, the workload may be lighter.

Another workload comes from the fact that you need to learn a whole new programming language, Solidity (Typescript-like), as well as a bit of BTC script.

It felt like 4 or more credit workload for me, an year 1 without comp 2711 / 3711 experience.

comment_content:
This course is about how to carry out various group activities (auction,lottery,voting ...) without trusting anyone and without leaking any more information than needed (no one needs to know that you have voted for Alice, they only need to know that your vote is valid).

Real world currencies like USD are controlled by the government, if the government doesn't like you they may freeze you bank account. They also know the details (who, when ...) of your transections so if the government gets hacked there'll be privacy issues. Cryptocurrencies attempts to solve this problem by removing the role of government (decentralization) and instead everyone is equal. However, as you probably noticed, there'll many issues e.g. Can Bob spends the $1 that Alice owns ? Can Alice deny the existence of a past transection ? You'll learn the concepts needed (digital signature, blockchain ...) to answer those questions in this course.

Once you have such a decentralized and secured cryptocurrencies, there're many things you can do like auctions, money laundering, gambling ... This course will teach you the techniques to design secured and privacy-preserving protocols for them. (Note: we're only interested in the theory, don't do illegal stuff IRL, you can still be caught like developers of Tornado Cash)

You'll also have the opportunity to implement those protocols using solidity programming language and simulate using VM, it's very fun !
comment_teaching:
Update (21stDec2024) : Amir is leaving HKUST (QS World 47) for Oxford (QS World 3), there's no surprise though as he's such an excellent researcher and teacher that everywhere welcomes him.

But to us HKUST students it's kind of a loss lol

Farewell Amir and best wishes for your continued success !

I'm not sure what'll become of this course in the future, may another great prof. continue its legacy.

-------------------------------

One of the best teachers I've ever met. Amir teaches by drawing and handwriting (with colors) on his digital notebook, this keeps my attention and helps me understand much better.

Before introducing a new concept, he'll first try to solve the problem with only old concepts and discuss the weaknesses of the approach. This ensures a smooth transition between topics and also motivates you to learn.

Amir is an expert and is also very active in this field, occasionally he will share some of his and TA's recent papers with us. I can see his passion both in research and teaching.

If you have difficulty understanding a concept or want clarification, feel free to email him. Typically he'll reply in the next working day and will provide helpful guidance.
comment_workload:
Quite challenging, you'll tasked with trying to develop perfect protocols (in terms of security, privacy, functionality, resistance to interrupt ...) You'll lose mark if TA can do much better than you.

So just try your best and apply what you've learned with creativity.
